TextENGNew: <The Houjou Family>
Each year, Oyashiro-sama's curse claims two victims.
Four consecutive years have now passed, bringing the total to eight -- and it is worth noting that half of them bore the Houjou name.
changeReason: Old vaguely says 'half of them have been members of this family'; new specifies 'half of them bore the Houjou name,' explicitly naming the family as in the JPN 北条姓を持つ.

TextENGNew: Note: In the second year's curse -- a certain fatal fall -- the dam proponent Mr. Houjou himself died, and his wife disappeared.@k(She was treated as having vanished under perilous circumstances, and declared dead the following year.)
In the fourth year's curse, the Houjou siblings' aunt, who served as their foster mother, was killed, and their elder brother disappeared.
The Houjou family was poor, and it would be generous to say Mr. Houjou's career was going well.
Apparently, there had been a plan to relocate to the mother's hometown, where family connections could help him find new employment.
changeReason: Old says 'upon reemployment via family connections, there was a plan to return to the mother's hometown'; new says 'plan to relocate to the mother's hometown, where family connections could help him find new employment.' The JPN 再就職をあてに indicates the move's purpose was to seek employment through connections at the destination, not that reemployment had already happened.

TextENGNew: For a man in Mr. Houjou's position, the eviction required by the Hinamizawa dam project -- along with the generous compensation it promised -- must have seemed like a godsend.
Mr. Houjou eagerly cooperated with the Ministry of Construction's negotiations from the earliest stages and was tapped to serve as the local representative of the pro-dam faction.
Rumors circulated that the Ministry of Construction had bought his support with money, though the truth remains unknown.
However, the dam's proponents were a small and vastly outnumbered minority.
Moreover, once the Sonozaki family consolidated its power base and began rallying the village against the dam, every pro-dam supporter except Mr. Houjou switched to the anti-dam side.
At that point, Hinamizawa was entirely united against the dam, and Mr. Houjou was singled out as a lackey of the dam project.
One could fairly say he was made into a scapegoat to solidify the anti-dam coalition.
changeReason: Old says 'used as a scapegoat for the anti-dam coalition'; new says 'made into a scapegoat to solidify the anti-dam coalition.' The JPN 反ダム結束の為の means 'for the purpose of anti-dam unity' — the scapegoating was done to strengthen unity, not just done on behalf of the coalition.

TextENGNew: In the end, the dam project collapsed with the first of Oyashiro-sama's curses -- the dismemberment murder of the construction foreman.
changeReason: Old vaguely says 'Oyashiro-sama's curse and the dismemberment incident'; new specifies 'the first of Oyashiro-sama's curses -- the dismemberment murder of the construction foreman.' The JPN 初めてのオヤシロさまの祟りである『現場監督殺人事件』 explicitly calls it the first curse and identifies the victim as the construction foreman.

TextENGNew: But retribution against the bitter enemies who supported the dam project continues to this day...
Today, scarcely anyone remains who supported the dam project or who carried a negative reputation from that era.
If any candidates for the curse remain, they are Teppei Houjou -- husband of the housewife beaten to death last year -- @kand Mr. Houjou's daughter, Satoko Houjou.
By a strange twist of fate, exactly two candidates remain.
Will this year's curse fall upon these two...?
There is more than sufficient reason to keep both of them under close observation......
